serving.natural_language_portion: One set of 9 dim sum pieces (5 steamed, 4 fried) intended for sharing among 4 people, estimated at approximately 450 g total (about 50 g per piece), yielding roughly 112 g per person.

nutrient_source.title: Dim Sum (mixed steamed and fried) — composite estimate based on HPB Singapore Foodo entries for har gow, siu mai, char siu bao, wu gok, and fried taro dumpling; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for comparable Chinese dumpling items
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a 9-piece mixed dim sum set. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated as a weighted composite of representative steamed dim sum (har gow, siu mai, steamed char siu bao, steamed rice roll cheung fun, steamed egg tart) and fried dim sum (wu gok/taro dumpling, spring roll, fried sesame ball, fried carrot cake) items from HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Steamed items weighted 5/9 (~56%) and fried items weighted 4/9 (~44%). Values represent a plausible central estimate for a typical Cantonese dim sum assortment as served in Singapore.

gi_source.title: Dimsum, steamed (Cantonese) — GI analogous estimate based on 'Dumplings, Chinese steamed' and 'Dim sum, steamed' entries; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a 9-piece mixed steamed-and-fried dim sum set. GI estimated at 52 as a weighted composite: steamed dim sum items (har gow, siu mai, cheung fun) have reported GI values of approximately 48–55 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables; fried dim sum items (wu gok, spring roll) are estimated at approximately 55–60 based on analogous fried wheat-dough pastry entries. Weighted average (5/9 steamed at GI ~50, 4/9 fried at GI ~55) yields approximately 52. gi_category set to 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. Glycemic load per serving calculated on a per-person basis (112 g share of 450 g total set): GL = 52 × (26.5 g/100g × 112 g) / 100 = 52 × 29.68 / 100 ≈ 15.43, rounded to 15.5. This per-person GL is reported for clinical relevance.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~470 mg per person share of ~112 g) from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoned fillings
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit dim sum consumption to 2–3 pieces per sitting and request low-sodium dipping sauces, as a typical per-person share of this set provides approximately 470 mg sodium, representing nearly 20% of the 2,400 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~52) and moderate carbohydrate content (~26.5 g per 100 g) from wheat and rice flour wrappers, with fried items contributing additional fat and energy density
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include dim sum in a meal plan in controlled portions (2–3 pieces), favouring steamed over fried varieties to reduce fat-driven postprandial glucose variability; the estimated GI of 52 is low, but the per-person glycemic load of approximately 15.5 warrants carbohydrate counting within the overall meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.8 g per 100 g) and trans fat (~0.2 g per 100 g) from fried items and lard-containing fillings, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit fried dim sum items and choose steamed varieties to reduce saturated fat and trans fat intake; the sodium content also warrants attention given its contribution to blood pressure elevation and cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dim Sum Set C (5 Steamed and 4 Fried, 4 Pax) is a Cantonese Chinese sharing platter comprising nine individual dim sum pieces — five steamed and four fried — served in a self-heating box to maintain temperature. Steamed items typically include har gow (shrimp dumplings in translucent rice-starch wrappers), siu mai (open-topped pork and shrimp dumplings), cheung fun (steamed rice noodle rolls with pork or shrimp), steamed char siu bao (fluffy steamed barbecue pork buns), and steamed egg tarts. Fried items typically include wu gok (crispy honeycomb taro dumplings with pork filling), spring rolls (crispy wheat pastry rolls with vegetable and pork filling), fried sesame balls (jin deui, glutinous rice dough filled with lotus paste and coated in sesame seeds), and fried carrot cake (chai tow kway, pan-fried radish cake). The set is designed for four people sharing, making it a popular choice for yum cha (Chinese tea brunch) gatherings. Nutritionally, the set provides a moderate energy density of approximately 238 kcal per 100 g, with a mixed macronutrient profile: protein from pork, shrimp, and chicken fillings; carbohydrates from wheat and rice flour wrappers; and fat from both the inherent fat in fillings and the oil absorbed during frying. The sodium content is notably high due to soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoned fillings, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The estimated glycemic index of 52 (low category) reflects the moderate glycemic impact of the wheat and rice flour wrappers, though fried items add fat that can modulate postprandial glucose response. This set is a culturally significant dish in Singaporean Chinese cuisine, commonly consumed during weekend dim sum brunches and family gatherings. It contains major allergens including gluten, crustaceans, soy, and sesame.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a 9-piece mixed steamed-and-fried dim sum set as sold by Loong Dim Sum Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of representative individual dim sum items from HPB Foodo (Singapore) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. The 5:4 steamed-to-fried ratio is taken from the product description. Actual values will vary depending on the specific items included, portion sizes, and restaurant preparation methods. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component items are well-documented in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate (5/9 steamed, 4/9 fried) derived from HPB Foodo Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual dim sum items. No single approved-source entry exists for this specific set. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy entries for dim sum items used in this composite. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary HPB Foodo source entries for these dim sum items. Similarly, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are set to -1 as they were not consistently reported or estimable from the composite source data. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed journal; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific set. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 4. Glycemic load of 15.5 is calculated on a per-person basis (112 g share of 450 g total set). 5. Halal status is not confirmed; pork is a primary ingredient in most dim sum fillings, making this item not halal. 6. The price of SGD 116.80 from field survey data is preserved from the input record for reference but is not used in nutritional calculations.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Established canonical food name, built full composite nutrient profile from HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central, computed GI analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, populated all mandatory multilingual names for Singaporean Chinese cuisine, built comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language with consistency check, populated all health context conditions with clinical guidance, wrote LLM-ready description exceeding 200 characters, documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg), verified aliases_by_language-to-flat-aliases consistency.

Iteration 2: Corrected q8 failure by ensuring all entries from aliases_by_language were present verbatim in the flat aliases list. Specifically added 'Yum Cha Set', 'Dim Sum Platter', 'Steamed Dim Sum', 'Fried Dim Sum', 'Har Gow and Siu Mai Set', 'Loong Dim Sum Set C', and 'Diǎnxīn tàocān' to the flat aliases list. Refined documentation for all -1 sentinel values in the 'notes' field for improved clarity and G3 compliance. Verified glycemic_load_per_serving is reported on per-person basis (15.5) for clinical relevance and documented calculation in gi_source notes; confirmed gi_category 'low' is correct for GI=52 per ≤55 threshold rule.
